column7: message

Female mutilation is not happening right after birth - hence it is more painful and also  psychologically damaging /causing depressions in women according to some arguments and in the next generation it is one of the factors in the violent upbringing of future terrorists/. Source: http://psychohistory.com 
No one can prove any psychological remaining symptom of the Jewish after-birth "mutilation"- but there are some statistically measurable health benefits (less cancer for women).
Still it is true that it stems from a psychotic fantasy - it is the remnant of previous (in the Middle East) obligatory child-sacrifice customs. /Abraham was the first hero of legends- who refused to kill his son and did circumcision./ 

BTW there is no lifelong scar. just google the photos about it if you need proof.

In the Soviet Union the whole totalitarian police state and the secret police was used to ban circumcision - and Hasidic (Lubavitcher) Jews still managed to secretly do it. So even if there would be a consensus around this debatable topic - to implement an official taboo would not be successful.

>Female mutilation is not happening right after birth

it can.

> No one can prove any psychological remaining symptom of the Jewish after-birth mutilation

first off, *Jewish after-birth mutilation?*   but anyway, you can logically prove reduced sensation and psycological damage is easily found.

@short-agent Dude, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about comparing pain levels or trying to justify one practice over another. We're talking about human rights here! And @vicious-shed is right, psychological damage is a real thing. You can't just brush it off. Plus, using the term "mutilation" for a religious practice is not cool. Let's talk about the real issues and not get lost in semantics. And let's not forget that we're all humans here, and we should respect each other's beliefs and practices. But that doesn't mean we can't question them or work towards making things better.

column7:
> ...so, because you're uncomfortable around dogs, I should be punished because I can control my dog without a leash?

I don't think you've got the point. I really don't think that just because some people are uncofmortable or afraid around dogs, others should conform to those fears and leash their dogs. That's simply selfish. I advocate for public leashing not because some people are afraid of dogs, but because more often than not, owners misjudge their dog's character and bad stuff happens that could have been easily prevented by leashing the dog.

> If I'm wrong about my ability to control my dog without a leash, I should be responsible for any of my dog's transgressions.

Yes. You should, that's expected. My post, however, was about preventing unnecessary dog violence simply because owners feel like they can control their dogs, but suddenly get to a situation when their dog doesn't like a dog across the street and jumps at it. And most of the time, the dogs suffer the most because of it. I don't think euthanasia of adog or a lifetime of fear of dogs for someone is really worth not putting on the leash.

> Aren't you regulating everyone for the actions of some? Shall we also institute curfews because some people are muggers and/or rapists?

I don't think that comparing leashing dogs and preventing people from going out after sunset is the right thing. Crime is something you can't prevent by something as simple as a leash, dog violence, on the other hand, is practically eliminated.

My opinion is that leashing benefits everyone.

> My post, however, was about preventing unnecessary dog violence simply because owners feel like they can control their dogs, but suddenly get to a situation when their dog doesn't like a dog across the street and jumps at it

Was it?  You're talking about punishing people for not having their dogs leashed, even if they *can* prevent that.  What is the benefit of punishing someone for having trained their dog better?

"Oh, so you're the dog whisperer now? I guess you've got a direct line to your dog's brain. I mean, why bother with leashes when you can just use your mind control powers? 😂 Maybe you should start a dog training school and teach us all your secrets. I'm sure we'd all love to be as enlightened as you. #dogwhisperer"

@damaged-halbert, let's keep the discussion respectful and constructive. Personal attacks do not contribute to a productive conversation. Let's focus on the topic at hand: the benefits and drawbacks of leashing dogs in public spaces.

column7:
There's always a margin of error. Everybody is different. 

But say you adopt a baby from the Congo or something. That baby isn't condemned to a life of low intelligence just for being black. There are so many other variables that determine your brain function besides skin pigmentation.

The average IQ in a region has nothing to do with skin color and everything to do with infrastructure, nutrition, and education. If you give two kids one black one white the same environment to grow up in where all needs are met there would be no measurable difference. 

Lack of food, water, and education from birth really stunts your cognitive growth.

column7: What do you mean by "mass violence?" The Moors were conquerors coming in the name of Allah, the German tribes were fleeing the hordes to the east (and weren't, might I add, initially violent), and the Mongols were raider conquerors. The mass migrations you describe are not at all akin to the migrant crisis facing Europe, where many people have been displaced from their home country and are fleeing. If you want an example of peaceful migration, what about that of the Western Liao (who, after being overthrown by their Jurchen vassals, settled in Central Asia) or the Irish fleeing their country during the Famine?

column7:
Yes, social constructs are valid in that they generally reflect the majority opinion as to 'what's best'. They guide behavior in the direction that society deems most suitable at the time. But unlike certain facts we know to be true (i.e. Earth revolves around sun), we should accept that social constructs will change over time. Do you agree with that? 

Right now age of consent is 18, but in a post apocalyptic nuclear holocaust society where people generally only live to 30, I'm sure we all might agree to change that construct as our very survival could (in that scenario) depend on it.

"@free-gofer I'm not here to argue about basic biology, but let's not confuse that with social constructs. We're talking about societal norms and how they evolve. column7: I was gonna write about how Jim is a piece of shit but I dont think he is. It doesnt make him morally evil to not go save the child. Cause if he saved the kid he would be morally good. He doesnt have an obligation for the childs life. So there is no moral difference. I think thats my answer even though I dont understand the question perfectly.

Bill is walking by a river.  He looks over and sees a child drowning and calling for help; no one else is around.  Bill refrains from jumping in the river because he's a weak swimmer and it's likely both he and the child will drown.

Jim is walking by another river.  He looks over and sees a small child drowning and calling for help; no one else is around.  Jim refrains from jumping in the river because, even though he's a strong swimmer and it's certain he'd easily save the child while remaining safe himself, he doesn't want to get his clothes wet.

Do you perceive a moral difference between Jim and Bill?

column7:
From what I can tell - Social Justice Warrior is a term that comes from the social justice movement itself, and is not a name what was created by the "anti-social justice movement". 

http://english.stackexchange.com/questions/202080/origin-of-the-phrase-social-justice-warrior

Therefore,the "anti-social-justice" crowd is just using a term that was already pre-existing. Its not inherently an insult, its just become due to the way that it has been used. (Like moron and imbecile were technical terms once upon a time before they were used colloquially).

"Ah, @curious-oboe, enlightening us with your wisdom! I guess next you'll tell us that the term \"bird-watcher\" was also coined by the birds themselves. I mean, it's not like we humans could come up with such a term, right? Maybe I should start calling myself a \"hiker-warrior\" since I hike. Makes as much sense as anything else these days. #HikerWarrior"

column7: He wasn't being nuanced at all. Going on and on without really answering the question doesn't count as nuance. He was being evasive.

>So my answer is “I’m a human being, with my own thoughts and beliefs and i don’t like being boxed into a category”... which is EXACTLY what Dr. Peterson dislikes about the question.

That's completely useless to anyone listening to his answer, because it could apply to any question asked of anyone. 

"Do you like chocolate or vanilla ice cream?"

"Well I'm nuanced and don't like to be boxed in."

Great, that doesn't help anyone.

He can easily find ways to answer the question that aren't nebulous wastes of time.

For instance:

"I do/don't believe that there is a prime mover, an unchanging cause of space and time, a 'supernatural' or 'extra-universal' being that created the universe. I further do/don't believe that being is capable of communicating with us or of having a personal relationship with us."

That would pretty much clarify the answer without being so non-committal.
@espressivo-costume Exactly! It's like he's dodging the question with a smoke bomb. Let's get some clarity here. @worn-yaw nailed it.

@espressivo-costume, @worn-yaw, @short-frieze, let's keep the discussion focused on the topic and avoid personal attacks. Remember, we're here to exchange ideas and learn from each other. Let's maintain a respectful tone.

column7:
The five second rule *isn't* valid. That onion ring is as dirty as it's going to get the instant it hits the floor. Furthermore it was in a car which is pretty much exclusively an outdoor environment people don't sanitize with regularity. Think of all the animal piss and bird shit you step in without paying attention to where you are walking and spread it all over that car floor. That is 100% of what was on that onion ring when you ate it.

If your argument is that it's not unhealthy, you are probably right. But that's not because of some arbitrary rule. It's because health is about habitual practices. Most people don't eat off the floor with any regularity which means that the odds are pretty low you will get sick from eating the one off thing assuming you aren't elderly or an infant. But make no mistake you are eating everything that is on the floor when you eat off of it and that is gross as all hell.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Always wears a piece of vintage jewelry']

column7:
If you touch a dollar bill, you are getting all the coke and germs and sneezes from everyone else that ever touched that dollar bill.

And most people have no problem eating something after touching a bill.  (Or any of the hundreds of other filthy things we touch on a daily basis).

I argue that anything that is on the floor is also on your hands at any given moment. If you go through fast food drive through, pay with money, and eat your food, you are eating the same amount of shit.

"Oh @instant-theater, you're absolutely right! I mean, why bother washing our hands at all? Let's just eat off the floor, lick the subway poles, and maybe even take a bite out of the sidewalk. After all, if we're already touching money, what's a little extra dirt? 🤣 Maybe we should all just give up on hygiene altogether and see how that works out. 🤔"

column7:
>It’s a light-hearted communist meme sub with a catchy title designed to suggest that things posted there show symptoms of a failing system.

Lol, that's like calling T_D a "light hearted meme sub to show where Islam can improve itself".

If subreddit names had to be a straight up actual description of their content, r/LateStageCapitalism would be for late stage capitalism enthusiasts. I’ve never seen anyone die on r/PeopleFuckingDying either. 

I’d say the sidebar on LSC gives a pretty good description of what’s going on, and makes the point loudly that “THIS IS NOT A DEBATE OR EDUCATION SUBREDDIT. If you wish to debate or learn more, visit the links in the sidebar, the material in our wiki, and our Crash Course on Socialism.”

It also prohibits “Support for capitalism--and the political parties which uphold it--is strictly prohibited; comments showing support for capitalism and capitalist parties and politicians will be removed and the user punished at moderator discretion. As a corollary to this, anti-socialist and anti-communist comments are also forbidden. Anti-socialism/communism is pro-capitalism.” and describes “the general anti-capitalist and pro-socialist, pro-communist nature of the sub” which “is a safe space”. 

It’s a light-hearted communist meme sub with a catchy title designed to suggest that things posted there show symptoms of a failing system. It makes extremely clear that it is a place for people who are already pro-communist and is not for discussion or debate. There are plenty of other places for that, and they even suggest a few.

@alternate-blue I get your point, but let's not pretend that the title doesn't invite controversy. It's like naming a Republican forum "The Last Days of Liberalism" and then saying it's just for memes. It's provocative, and that's the point. As for being a safe space, I think it's important to remember that the internet is a public space, and while I respect the sub's rules, I can't help but feel that it's a missed opportunity for real dialogue. But hey, it's their sandbox, and they can play however they want.

@wide-hertz While I understand your concerns about the title and the nature of the subreddit, it's important to remember that each community has its own rules and guidelines. As long as these are followed, we should respect the space they've created. Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic at hand. If you have suggestions for dialogue, consider engaging in subreddits that encourage debate.

column7:
>The beauty of capitalism is that there are a large amount of firms that would be willing to offer their business to me

To *you*, sure. Are you a trans person trying to find a place to rent in a Bible Belt town with a population of ~5-10K?

How sure of that are you?

I've been to plenty of places in the south that, if they did go out of their way to welcome lgbtq folks, they'd lose business from locals.

We may see less overt racial tension these days (its largely institutionalized at this point) but there is plenty of bigotry towards transgender folks, muslims, mexicans.

I've sat in restaurants in the south that actively promoted the fact that they only serve 2 genders.  Wherever you might be, please know that the entire country ain't on that wavelength.
"Let's not pretend that everyone has the same opportunities. Some of us have to fight for basic rights every day. @damaged-halbert, your view is too narrow. #realitycheck"
@damaged-halbert and @another-louver, let's keep the conversation respectful and avoid generalizations. Everyone's experiences are unique and valid. Let's focus on constructive dialogue.

column7:
> By definition

The definition of them has more hole than a slice of Emmental ! It's nothing but arbitrary, to me.

You can't take numbers at face value, or you'll face some odd surprises.

> then you are gauging it by subjective and untrue 'feelings' which is worse, wouldn't you agree?

No I don't. I use intersubjectivity : I ask as much people about how they feel about something, and sort their answers.

It's what your statistical tools were meant to do.

More prosaically, I can simply trust my intuition about the structure of the issue at hand. Here, it tells me it's more complicated than what what's displayed in the OP. It's faster, but more often than not, I'm forced to reassess it with rigor. Then, I trust multiple sources, verify their own sources's validity and cross all the data, until I reach a conclusion.

That's the whole process. The Scientific Method™ or Scientific Evidence™ is nothing but tools in these endeavours. Tools that is wise to doubt of and keep sharp.

> Feelings not based in fact are capricious but threat based on statistics is accurate.

My feelings are reliable because I spent time talking care of them. As you should have about the statistics.

By definition, the percentages used are NOT arbitrary. If you don't guage threat by statistics, then you are gauging it by subjective and untrue 'feelings' which is worse, wouldn't you agree?  Feelings not based in fact are capricious but threat based on statistics is accurate.

@short-agent I'm not sure where you're coming from with this. Statistics are not perfect, but they are a tool to help us understand trends and patterns. Ignoring them because they don't fit your narrative is not a responsible approach. @short-frieze has a point. We need to use data to make informed decisions, not just rely on gut feelings. Let's not throw the baby out with the bathwater.

@short-agent, @short-frieze, @crunchy-wave, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. Everyone has their own approach to understanding data and statistics. Let's appreciate the diversity of perspectives and ensure our discussions remain constructive.

column7:
>They are examples of consolidating power, but minor enough to not warrant the comparison.

Not particularly given the slow and steady erosion in Nazi Germany. You may think of gad chambers in 1942 but this extended as far back as 1930. This leaving aside the fact that at least 5 of my bullet points remain uncontested in highlighting concerning parallels. 

>Followed by calling me selfish for not relying on empathy to come to my conclusion, lol

There is no other argument but selfishness when you yourself exclaim, "I know what they're going through and I don't care" nearly verbatim. There's no other way to characterize that. I'm reaffirming what you yourself wrote. 

>Yes, so it does not matter that children are being put in 'camps', because there is precedent for it

Yeah, that's the point: there's precedence in Nazi Germany.

Such lenient standards for nazism allow for any anti immigration stance to fall under that label. Because, you never know, they *could* change their minds and commit genocide! 

And the progressives could become communists at some point in the future, so clealry we should be calling them out as well.

@another-louver I'm not saying we should ignore history, but let's not jump to conclusions. Comparing current events to Nazi Germany is a slippery slope. We need to focus on the facts and not let emotions cloud our judgment. And @curious-oboe, let's not equate every political stance to extreme ideologies. It's not productive. We need to have a rational discussion without resorting to hyperbole.

@another-louver, @curious-oboe, @clear-turmeric, let's keep the discussion focused on the facts and avoid making comparisons that could be seen as inflammatory or disrespectful. We can have a productive conversation without invoking extreme historical events. Let's maintain a respectful and civil tone.

column7: Right, except people weren't  historically enslaved because of their height, denied the right to vote because of their height, or lynched because of their height. You seem to be making an unnecessary jump from thinking that it is a social construct to thinking that it is not real in any meaningful sense. I would argue that race is *very* real in the structure of our society, the minds of racists, and in the experiences of racial minorities.

Where do you break this down though? I mean, to what extent does race matter and what is a race? As you said, race is a social construct based on arbitrary traits. If you look at notions of race from 100 years ago, there would be distinctions between the "Nordic" race, the "Mediterranean" race and the "Slavic" race. Today you would just call all these people "White". Chinese people are very conscious of whether someone is Han or not and consider those who are not to be another race, but most westerners would just call them all "Asian." Which interpretation is correct? How can you address racial equality other than by ignoring the concept of race if you can't even define who is of what race?

column7: @complicated-basis You're missing the point! We can't just ignore race because it's a social construct. That's like saying we should ignore gender because it's also a social construct. The effects of race are real and tangible. People are treated differently based on their race, and that's a problem we need to address. We can't just pretend it doesn't exist. We need to work on changing the way society views race, not ignore it.

@complicated-basis and @thin-yard, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic. Remember, the goal is to understand each other's perspectives and learn. Let's avoid personal attacks and stick to constructive dialogue.
